wifi_sta_confirm_msg:
Device will restart and use Station mode only.

• Saved networks are required
• Reconnects use retry backoff
• Access Point will not start automatically if STA fails
wifi_ap_confirm_msg:
Device will restart and use Access Point mode only.

• Station reconnects will stop
• Connect to the AP SSID to reconfigure
wifi_off_confirm_msg:
Device will restart with the WiFi radio off.

• Internet, Telegram, and local WiFi access will stop
• Use Matrix menu or factory reset to enable WiFi again
wifi_sta_no_saved: No saved networks. Scan to add one.
imu_live_monitor_desc: Keeps IMU sampling active for this page.
imu_alarm_monitor_desc: Reserves IMU sampling for alarm evaluation.
macros_delete_msg: Are you sure you want to delete "{name}"? This action cannot be undone.
jiggler_desc_keyboard: Simulates key press (F13) to prevent sleep without moving cursor.
help_intro_body: Use this page as a starting point for the most important areas of the device. Begin with WiFi, confirm the device is healthy on System Status, then set up alarms and notifications.
help_intro_step_1: Open WiFi Station and add your home or office network if the device is still running in Access Point mode.
help_intro_step_2: Open System Status to confirm the device is online, connected, and reading sensors normally.
help_intro_step_3: Configure at least one notification channel, then review the Alarms and Charts pages to confirm thresholds and current readings.
help_intro_note: After a factory reset, or when there are no saved WiFi networks, the device starts in Access Point mode. Open {hostname} or {ip} to configure it.
help_alarm_flow_body: Sensor readings are compared against the configured thresholds. When a value crosses a limit, the alarm becomes visible on the device and can also trigger local and remote actions.
help_alarm_flow_point_1: Start on the Alarms page to review thresholds for temperature, humidity, and CO2.
help_alarm_flow_point_2: Use Charts to decide whether a reading is a short spike or a longer trend before changing the threshold.
help_alarm_flow_point_3: Start with an LED alarm for a quick local test, then add Telegram, Pushover, or Webhook notifications for remote delivery.
help_alarm_flow_note: Tip: after changing a notification channel, send a test or trigger a simple threshold condition to confirm delivery before relying on it.
help_troubleshooting_body: If the device does not behave as expected, these are the first checks worth doing.
help_troubleshooting_issue_1_title: I cannot open the web interface
help_troubleshooting_issue_1_desc: Connect to the device Access Point and open matrixhub.local or 192.168.4.1. After saving WiFi credentials, the address may change to one assigned by your router.
help_troubleshooting_issue_2_title: I do not receive remote notifications
help_troubleshooting_issue_2_desc: Check whether a notification channel is configured correctly, send a test from that page, and confirm the alarm threshold really triggered. Review Data Logs and System Status if messages still do not appear.
help_troubleshooting_issue_3_title: The device returned to Access Point mode
help_troubleshooting_issue_3_desc: This usually means the saved WiFi network is unavailable or the credentials changed. Reconnect through the Access Point and update the WiFi Station settings.
help_troubleshooting_issue_4_desc: Compare the live values with Charts, verify sensor placement, and use Compensation only when the offset is stable and repeatable.
help_quick_links_body: Use these shortcuts for the most common setup, diagnostic, and maintenance tasks.
help_section_connectivity_body: Configure how the device connects to your network and where the web interface is available.
help_section_alerts_body: Review thresholds, live readings, historical charts, and sensor compensation options.
help_section_notifications_body: Connect the device to Telegram, Pushover, Webhooks, or heartbeat monitoring.
help_section_integrations_body: Manage local integrations such as Shelly devices, Bluetooth sensors, and WiFi sensing tools.
help_section_system_body: Inspect system health, logs, time sync, display behavior, user management, and maintenance pages.
help_section_usb_body: Configure Air Mouse behavior and the USB input tools exposed by the device.
sleep_confirm_msg:
Device will enter deep sleep mode and wake automatically every 10 minutes to:
• Read sensors
• Log data to storage
• Check for activity

Sleep cycle continues until you interact with the device (button press or web UI access).

This mode is ideal for battery-powered operation.

auth_error_session_init_failed: Could not start your session. Please try signing in again.
auth_notice_time_changed: Device time was corrected. Sign in again to continue.
access_admin_required_desc: This page contains settings that can change device behavior. Sign in with an administrator account to view and edit them.
ap_apply_msg:
ESP32 will restart to apply the new Access Point configuration.

• If in AP mode, reconnect using new credentials
• SSID and password will be updated
• Network settings will take effect after restart
charts_no_data: No data available yet. Charts will appear after first log entry (5 minutes).

alarms_how_work_2: Alarms are evaluated whenever new sensor data arrives and the UI is updated in real time

alarms_no_rules_hint: Click "Add Rule" to create your first alarm. You can configure up to {max} rules.

logs_desc: Logs include: CO₂, temperature and humidity.
menu_locked_security: Enable security to unlock this page

power_dialog_reset_msg:
This will be erased:
• WiFi configuration (saved STA networks)
• LittleFS data (logs/data logger and configuration files)
• App preferences (settings)

Restored to defaults after reboot:
• AP defaults:
- SSID: matrixhub.local
- Password: (open)
- IP: 192.168.4.1
• Hostname / mDNS:
- Hostname: matrixhub
- Address: https://matrixhub.local

Device will enter deep sleep mode and wake automatically every 10 minutes to:
• Read sensors
• Log data to storage
• Check for activity

Sleep cycle continues until you interact with the device (button press or web UI access).

This mode is ideal for battery-powered operation.

network_diag_http_description: These counters track real HTTP(S) sockets. A WebSocket upgrade reuses the same socket, so successful WS handshakes and channel subscribe/unsubscribe traffic should not increase them.
network_diag_unavailable: Network diagnostics are not available yet.
airmouse_enable_hint: Enable Sensor Movement or Sensor Click to see live data.
airmouse_click_help_intro: These settings control how taps or the physical button are interpreted. Actions are executed after the click window expires.
airmouse_click_help_click_enabled_desc: Turns all click/tap actions on or off.
airmouse_click_help_click_source_desc: Sensor Tap uses IMU taps, Button uses the physical button. Both use the same single/double/triple actions.
airmouse_click_help_tap_threshold_desc: Higher values require a stronger tap and reduce false triggers; lower values increase sensitivity.
airmouse_click_help_debounce_desc: Minimum time between tap detections. Too low can double-count a single impact; too high can miss fast sequences.
airmouse_click_help_click_speed_desc: Time window that groups 1/2/3 taps or clicks. Larger window makes double/triple easier but delays the single action.
airmouse_click_help_actions_desc: Choose what happens for each gesture: mouse click or script.
airmouse_click_help_script_desc: Executes a macro script from the list. Requires macros enabled in the Macros section.
airmouse_macro_requires_macros: Running a script requires enabled Macros.
airmouse_click_help_note: Tip: after unification, single tap/click waits for the click window to expire.
airmouse_cursor_help_intro: These settings control cursor movement. Start with sensitivity, then tune deadzone and acceleration.
airmouse_cursor_help_movement_enabled_de: Turns cursor motion on or off.
airmouse_cursor_help_sensitivity_desc: Higher values move faster. Use X/Y to balance horizontal and vertical speed.
airmouse_cursor_help_deadzone_desc: Small gyro motions inside the deadzone are ignored. Increase to reduce jitter.
airmouse_cursor_help_acceleration_desc: Above the speed threshold, higher values move the cursor farther for the same motion.
airmouse_cursor_help_calibrate_desc: Run calibration while the device is still to remove drift.
airmouse_cursor_help_note: Tip: if fast moves feel curved, recalibrate and reduce acceleration.
airmouse_msg_calib_cooldown: Too many calibration attempts. Try again in a moment.
matrix_menu_enabled_desc: Keep enabled when WiFi mode should be recoverable from the matrix menu.
matrix_menu_text_color_desc: Color used for menu text on the matrix.
matrix_auto_desc: Use the IMU orientation instead of a fixed screen rotation.
matrix_effects_disabled_hint: The saved effect setup is kept and will apply when effects are enabled.
matrix_effect_mode_live_desc: Saved with the card Save action.
matrix_effect_palettes_desc: Apply a ready-made 3-color palette before fine-tuning below.
matrix_admin_desc: You need administrator privileges to modify hardware settings.
keyboard_error_disabled: Keyboard is disabled. Enable it and restart the device first.
keyboard_disabled_notice_body: Enable the keyboard feature and save with restart to use the live keyboard panel.
styles_alert_info: Info: This is how alerts look.
webhook_url_invalid: Please enter a valid HTTP or HTTPS URL
settings_error_invalid_request: The device rejected the request because the payload was invalid.
settings_error_persist_failed: The device could not save the settings.
settings_error_request_too_large: The request was too large for the device to process.
settings_error_restore_failed: The device could not restore the previous settings after the failed update.
settings_error_update_failed: The device could not apply the settings.
pushover_desc_box: Send real-time notifications to your Android, iOS, and Desktop devices using Pushover.

matrix_custom_icons_desc: Used for info, warning, and critical alarms when Icon mode is selected.
comp_enabled_desc: Automatically corrects temperature readings based on device heat (CPU).
comp_disabled_desc: No correction. Readings might be higher due to device self-heating.
comp_base_offset_desc: Subtract this amount when CPU is at reference temperature.
comp_ref_cpu_desc: CPU temperature where Base Correction applies.
comp_slope_desc: How much to increase correction as CPU gets warmer (deg correction per 1°C CPU rise).
comp_min_offset_desc: Correction will never be less than this.
comp_max_offset_desc: Correction will never exceed this security limit.
usb_terminal_target_port_desc: Serial port on the target device for output redirection (e.g. /dev/ttyUSB0).
usb_terminal_idle_timeout_desc: Delay before the service considers a command output complete and forwards data.
usb_terminal_quick_scripts_requires_macr: Running quick scripts requires enabled Macros.
usb_terminal_quick_scripts_run_error: Failed to start the selected script.
usb_terminal_quick_scripts_stop_error: Failed to stop the running script.
usb_terminal_disable_requires_cancel: Stop the active terminal session before disabling USB Terminal.
usb_terminal_console_requires_security: USB terminal console requires security to be enabled.
usb_terminal_parse_error: Failed to parse USB terminal response.
usb_terminal_message_disabled_service: USB Terminal Service is disabled. Enable it in the Web UI.
usb_terminal_message_busy_other_session: Terminal is busy with another session.
usb_terminal_message_busy_own_session: Terminal is busy processing the previous command. Use status or cancel.
usb_terminal_message_internal_buffer: Internal error: failed to allocate terminal output buffer.
usb_terminal_message_internal_command: Internal error: failed to type the command.
usb_terminal_message_port_not_set: Port not set. Typing blindly without feedback.
usb_terminal_message_running_no_output: Command is running, but no output has been captured yet.

livetail_error_clear: Failed to clear live tail buffer.
help_contextual_modals_body: On selected screens, look for the help icon in the card header or a small Help button above the page content. Those modals explain local settings without forcing you to leave the current workflow.
dashboard_help_intro: The dashboard is a quick status view. It helps you spot trends and whether integrations are alive without opening each configuration page.
dashboard_help_how_body: Sensor cards show the current value and a short history. Integration widgets summarize the last known state and open the full page when selected.
dashboard_help_setup_body: Hover over sparklines on desktop to read exact values and times. When something needs tuning, open the related page such as Charts, Alarms, Bluetooth, or Wi-Fi Sensing.
dashboard_help_watch_body: An empty card usually means the first sample has not arrived yet, not necessarily a failure. Warning icons or stale widgets are a sign to check system status, Wi-Fi, or logs.
wifi_sta_help_intro: This help covers the whole station page: hostname, saved networks, scan flow, and addressing.
wifi_sta_help_how_body: The device tries saved networks from top to bottom and follows the selected connection mode. In the hostname field, enter only the host name, for example matrixhub; the matrixhub.local address is built automatically by appending .local.
wifi_sta_help_setup_body: Keep the most trusted network first, use scan to prefill SSID, and leave static IP off unless your LAN really needs fixed addressing. Save only when you know how you will reconnect afterward.
wifi_sta_help_watch_body: Wrong static IP, gateway, or DNS values can make the device disappear from the network. In Station mode the device stays STA-only, so use router DHCP leases, the configured static address, Matrix menu, or factory reset to regain access.
wifi_ap_help_intro: The access point settings define the local network used when WiFi Mode is set to Access Point.
wifi_ap_help_how_body: SSID, password, channel, and local network values control the network clients join when AP mode is active. Hidden SSID only removes the broadcast and is not a security feature by itself.
wifi_ap_help_setup_body: Use a simple SSID and memorable password, keep channel and client limits conservative, and change IP settings only if they must fit another subnet. Defaults are the safest choice for first-run setup.
wifi_ap_help_watch_body: Changing AP IP settings can lock you out of Access Point mode just as easily as wrong STA settings. Avoid hidden SSID or unusual subnets unless you have documented them for later recovery.
ble_help_intro: Bluetooth scanning adds nearby BLE sensors and keeps their latest readings available for the dashboard and alarms.
ble_help_how_body: When scanning is enabled, the device looks for supported advertisements, you add selected sensors from the scan modal, and the dashboard updates after packets arrive. RSSI shows link quality, while battery and last-seen help estimate sensor health.
ble_help_setup_body: Enable scanning first, run a scan close to the sensor, add the right MAC, then wait for the first advertisement before deciding whether it works. Use aliases to make dashboards and alarms easier to read.
ble_help_watch_body: Low battery, weak RSSI, long beacon intervals, or moving the sensor too far away can make data look stale. A sensor can be saved correctly and still need some time before the first reading appears.
sensing_help_intro: Wi-Fi sensing estimates activity from signal variance on the current STA connection, so it works best on a stable network path.
sensing_help_how_body: Sampling interval controls how often signal data is checked, and threshold decides how much variance counts as motion. Lower thresholds react faster, while higher thresholds ignore more background noise.
sensing_help_setup_body: Start with moderate threshold and interval values, save, and observe the live card for a few quiet minutes and during real movement. Tune one slider at a time so you can tell what changed.
sensing_help_watch_body: Crowded Wi-Fi, weak RSSI, or unstable STA links can look like motion. If detection is too nervous, raise the threshold or slow the interval; if it misses movement, lower the threshold gradually.
csi_help_intro: CSI is the more detailed live view of Wi-Fi channel behavior and is best used when regular Wi-Fi sensing is not enough.
csi_help_how_body: The amplitude chart shows live subcarrier activity, while RSSI, gain, and stream state describe signal conditions and whether capture is active. Use it for fine-grained diagnostics rather than as a simple presence toggle.
csi_help_setup_body: Turn the stream on only when the STA connection is stable, then compare a quiet baseline against real motion in the room. Use regular Wi-Fi sensing for everyday tuning and CSI when you need deeper diagnostics.
csi_help_watch_body: CSI is sensitive to unstable links and can stay in connecting state until the stream is ready. High noise or poor RSSI can make the chart hard to interpret, so verify the normal Wi-Fi sensing view first.
shelly_help_intro: Shelly devices let the system expose smart relay state on the dashboard and trigger actions from alarms.
shelly_help_how_body: Each entry stores the device IP, generation, and relay index the firmware should control. Online state, power, and temperature are fetched from the configured device when it responds.
shelly_help_setup_body: Reserve or document the Shelly IP, pick the correct generation, and keep relay index aligned with the actual output you want to switch. Use a clear name so alarm rules and dashboard cards stay readable.
shelly_help_watch_body: If the device IP changes, the dashboard will show it offline even though the Shelly still works. Wrong generation or relay index can make commands fail or toggle the wrong output.
pushover_help_intro: Pushover sends alarm and test notifications to the Pushover service using your application token and user key.
pushover_help_how_body: After enabling the integration, saved credentials are used for outgoing messages from the device. Test delivery confirms that both the token and the target user are accepted.
pushover_help_setup_body: Create or reuse a Pushover application, paste the app token and user key, save, and send a test before relying on alarms. Keep credentials copied exactly as shown in the Pushover dashboard.
pushover_help_watch_body: A valid-looking token can still fail if it targets the wrong app or user. If tests fail, check copied values, network reachability, and whether the Pushover account can receive messages.
heartbeat_help_intro: Heartbeat periodically calls the configured URLs so external systems can confirm the device is alive.
heartbeat_help_how_body: Each slot can point to a different endpoint and is triggered on schedule when enabled. The optional insecure setting relaxes TLS validation for problematic HTTPS targets.
heartbeat_help_setup_body: Name slots by destination, paste full URLs, save, and use the test panel to confirm the remote system receives them. Enable insecure mode only for endpoints you trust and cannot fix otherwise.
heartbeat_help_watch_body: Missing paths, redirects, TLS problems, or strict firewalls often look like generic delivery failures. Keep only the slots you really use enabled so diagnostics stay readable.
udp_help_intro: UDP streaming sends periodic sensor snapshots to another host without waiting for an HTTP response.
udp_help_how_body: Host and port choose the receiver, format controls the payload shape, and interval sets how often packets are emitted. This is lightweight and useful for collectors on the same network.
udp_help_setup_body: Start with a reachable local host, pick the format your receiver expects, save, and test before leaving it enabled long term. Use slower intervals first if the receiver is experimental or heavily logged.
udp_help_watch_body: UDP does not guarantee delivery, so missing packets can be normal on noisy networks. Wrong host, wrong port, or mismatched parser expectations are the most common setup issues.
ntp_help_intro: Time settings decide whether the device keeps time from NTP or from a manually entered value, and that choice affects logs, charts, and alarms.
ntp_help_how_body: With NTP enabled, the device syncs from the configured server and uses the selected timezone label for local display. Manual mode stores a local timestamp without network sync.
ntp_help_setup_body: Prefer NTP whenever the device has network access, choose the correct timezone label, save, and verify local and UTC time in the status card. Use browser time only as a fallback or for isolated setups.
ntp_help_watch_body: Wrong timezone makes local timestamps confusing even when UTC is correct. Manual time drifts over time, so logs and scheduled behavior become less trustworthy if NTP stays off.
power_help_intro: Power configuration controls automatic sleep behavior and how long the device waits before sleeping again after boot.
power_help_how_body: Autosleep watches inactivity, the grace timer protects the device right after startup, and wake interval determines when the device can reappear. These settings trade convenience against availability and power savings.
power_help_setup_body: Start with conservative timeouts, save, and confirm that normal administration still fits inside the grace window. Keep autosleep off if the device is meant to stay continuously reachable.
power_help_watch_body: Too aggressive sleep settings can make remote debugging and reconfiguration frustrating. Before tightening timeouts, make sure you have a reliable wake path and a recovery workflow.
file_manager_help_intro: File Manager exposes device storage for inspection, download, upload, and deletion from the web interface.
file_manager_help_how_body: The browser reads the current LittleFS directory, while uploads and deletions change stored files immediately. Some paths are intentionally read-only to protect config or system data.
file_manager_help_setup_body: Download important files before replacing them, upload only known-good assets, and prefer small, targeted changes over bulk cleanup. Use the storage card to keep an eye on remaining space.
file_manager_help_watch_body: Deleting or replacing the wrong file can remove custom content or break related features. Protected paths block some operations, but this view should still be treated like an admin tool, not a casual browser.
charts_help_intro: Charts show historical sensor data parsed from stored log files, not the live values already visible on the dashboard.
charts_help_how_body: The date selector loads available days from storage and each chart plots the parsed samples for that day. Empty days usually mean there was no retained log for that date or the file could not provide usable data.
charts_help_setup_body: Use the latest day first, then compare problem periods against logs, alarms, and current dashboard behavior. If you are troubleshooting trends, keep the chart date aligned with the log month you inspect.
charts_help_watch_body: A blank chart is not always a frontend bug; it can also mean missing history, an empty fallback file, or a not-found day. Live conditions may differ from stored history, so avoid tuning thresholds from old data alone.
logs_help_intro: Data logs are the retained history behind charts and long-term troubleshooting views.
logs_help_how_body: Months group binary files written by the device, while the statistics cards summarize storage usage and coverage. Downloading a log keeps a copy outside the device so you can inspect or archive it safely.
logs_help_setup_body: Use logs when you need retention, correlation, or evidence of past sensor behavior. Download important months before deleting anything, especially if you are investigating intermittent problems.
logs_help_watch_body: Deleting logs saves space but permanently removes historical context for charts and debugging. Large gaps usually point to retention limits, storage pressure, or periods when data was not recorded.
